subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content: Core components from the official rulebook: 15 character cards total (3 copies each of 5 roles: Duke, Assassin, Contessa, Captain, Ambassador), 50 coins, and summary cards. Optional rules component: Inquisitor role variant that replaces Ambassador (used only if all players agree to that variant).

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content: Shuffle the 15-character deck. Deal each player 2 face-down character cards (their influence). Each player also takes 2 coins from the treasury. Place remaining coins in a central treasury and place remaining character cards face-down as the Court deck. Players keep their own influence cards hidden; face-down cards represent active influence.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content: Choose a starting player by any agreed fair method and proceed clockwise. On each turn, a player chooses exactly one action: either a basic action (Income, Foreign Aid, Coup) or any one character action (even if they may not actually have that character).

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content: 1) Start of turn check: if you have 10 or more coins, you must take the Coup action. 2) Declare exactly one action (basic or character action claim). 3) Other players may challenge if the action or block depends on a claimed character. 4) Resolve challenge if made: if claimant cannot reveal required character, claimant loses 1 influence; if claimant reveals correctly, challenger loses 1 influence and claimant shuffles revealed card into deck then draws a replacement. 5) Resolve the action effect (or block) if still valid. 6) Any player who loses influence turns one character face-up of their choice; face-up influence is dead and unusable. 7) Turn passes clockwise.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content: Basic actions: Income = take 1 coin from treasury (cannot be blocked). Foreign Aid = take 2 coins from treasury (can be blocked by Duke). Coup = pay 7 coins, choose an opponent; that opponent immediately loses 1 influence (if you have 10+ coins, this action is mandatory on your turn). Character actions and blocks: Duke action = take 3 coins from treasury; Duke block = block Foreign Aid. Assassin action = pay 3 coins to attempt assassination of another player’s influence; Contessa can block an assassination against herself. Captain action = take 2 coins from another player (steal); Captain can block stealing against themselves; Ambassador can also block stealing against themselves. Ambassador action = draw 2 cards from Court deck, choose which (if any) to exchange with your current face-down cards, then return 2 cards to Court deck. Important claim rule: you may declare any character action whether or not you actually have that role, but such claims are challengeable.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content: Influence and elimination: each player starts with 2 influence (2 face-down cards). Whenever you lose influence, reveal one card face-up. Face-up cards cannot be used for actions or blocks. If both your cards are face-up, you are eliminated. Challenge system: character-based actions and character-based blocks automatically succeed unless challenged. If challenged unsuccessfully, challenger loses influence. If challenged successfully, claimant loses influence. When a truthful claimant proves the character, that revealed card is shuffled back into deck and replaced with a fresh hidden card, preserving secrecy. Optional Inquisitor variant (replacing Ambassador): Inquisitor can draw 1 card and optionally exchange with one hidden card, OR force an opponent to show one chosen hidden card (opponent chooses which) and may force redraw/shuffle-back sequence; Inquisitor also blocks stealing.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content: Coup has no point scoring system. The game ends immediately when only one player still has at least one face-down influence card. That player is the winner. There are no additional victory points and no tiebreakers because final survival determines victory.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content: Build a believable identity early: repeat a coherent role story rather than making random contradictory claims each turn. Track coin thresholds at the table—especially who is near 3 (assassination) and 7 (coup). Maintain enough economy that you are not forced into weak lines, but avoid becoming the obvious immediate target. If you plan to bluff aggressively, do it where failure costs least (early) rather than in must-win late turns.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content: 1) Challenging too often on weak reads. Every failed challenge costs influence and can lose the game quickly. 2) Ignoring mandatory Coup at 10+ coins. New players accidentally attempt other actions illegally. 3) Bluffing every turn with different roles. Inconsistent claims make you easy to challenge. 4) Failing to count treasury and player coins. Good Coup play is threshold management, not just social reads. 5) Revealing influence without future plan. When forced to lose influence, reveal the card that preserves your strongest remaining bluff/block options.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content: Decision 1: Tempo vs threat profile—whether to accelerate coins quickly (strong economy, higher table heat) or stay modest (lower attention, slower control). Decision 2: Challenge timing—do you challenge now to break an opponent’s momentum, or hold influence and wait for a higher-value challenge point? Decision 3: Assassination pressure vs guaranteed coup value—paying 3 for immediate pressure can be great, but saving toward guaranteed coups can be safer in uncertain bluff states.

metadata.notes: Written from provided Coup official rulebook text, including optional Inquisitor variant note.
